recommendation.justification: The target price of VND 29,600/share is based on a fair P/B of 1.4x. The target price was raised by 5% after shareholders approved the HoSE listing, which led to the removal of a 5% valuation discount previously applied due to the bank being listed on HNX and thus not included in ETF indices.

investment_thesis: ACB is considered a very attractive stock due to a rare combination of attractive valuation, good business growth prospects, and a strong balance sheet, featuring best-in-class asset quality and a high NPL coverage ratio.
The bank is one of the most cautious in Vietnam and possesses the best mortgage-backed retail banking model, with 90% of its loans being secured.

ACB is well-positioned to outperform other listed banks, especially during the challenging COVID-19 period, thanks to its competitive advantages: a large proportion of individual and household loans (supporting NIM, optimizing risk diversification, and curbing new NPL formation), a low-risk appetite (limited exposure to vulnerable sectors like construction, real estate, tourism-hospitality, and transportation), a strong balance sheet, and excellent risk management.
Despite a potentially low earnings growth outlook for 2020, ACB's business results are projected to recover well in 2021.
The upcoming listing on HoSE is expected to be a game-changer for ACB, fostering positive sentiment for the stock this year, with potential inclusion in ETF indices after 6 months of trading on HoSE.
An exclusive bancassurance deal anticipated this year is expected to generate extraordinary income and a stable stream of recurring revenue in the future.
The divestment from ACBS is also identified as a factor that will support ACB's stock price.
company_update: ACB targets a low single-digit profit before tax (PBT) growth for 2020, aiming for VND 7,636 billion (+2.0% y/y), which is close to BVSC's previous forecast of VND 7,741 billion (+3.0% y/y).

Key growth targets for 2020 include: total assets growth of +12.0% y/y, credit growth of +11.75% y/y (equivalent to the initial credit limit allowed by the State Bank of Vietnam), total deposits growth of +12.0% y/y, and maintaining the NPL ratio below 2.0%, with management confident it can be controlled below 1.0%.

The stock dividend policy for FY19/20 is expected to be 30%/18% respectively, with the 2019 dividend payment anticipated in September 2020. Management believes there is potential to increase the 2020 dividend, subject to SBV regulations.

Shareholders approved the HoSE listing, which management expects to execute after the 2019 stock dividend payment. Upon completion, ACB is projected to be included in indices such as VN30 (4% weight), VNDiamond (10%), VNFIN Select (12%), and VNFIN Lead (12%), though eligibility for ETF inclusion will be after 6 months of HoSE trading.

In the first 5 months of 2020, credit growth was 4.0%, slower than 10% and 8% in 5M18 and 5M19, respectively, but still outperformed the industry average of 1.96%. Management is confident in achieving the full-year credit growth target.

Consolidated PBT for 5M20 reached approximately VND 3.5 trillion (45.8% of the FY20 PBT target). Net fee and commission income was low (down about 20% y/y) due to COVID-19, but bancassurance and card segments showed growth, offsetting the decline.
The exclusive bancassurance deal remains a key factor. Management asserts ACB's competitive position to secure a favorable contract due to its retail focus and loyal, high-income customer base.

Loans affected by COVID-19 amount to approximately VND 15 trillion, representing 5.6% of outstanding loans at the end of 2019. ACB estimates about VND 600 billion in interest income will not be recognized this year but is expected to be recovered next year.
For FY20, management expects NIM to narrow by 30-50 basis points as ACB offers more preferential low-interest loans to stimulate credit demand and strengthen its loyal customer base.

ACB's strategy in response to COVID-19 challenges is to continue rotating loans towards the individual loan segment and reduce lending to SMEs this year, aligning with BVSC's view that a larger proportion of individual loans supports NIM, optimizes risk diversification, and curbs new NPL formation.

Management explained the early recognition of 2020 employee bonus expenses for the high Cost-to-Income Ratio (CIR) in Q1 2020, which BVSC believes will help reduce CIR in subsequent quarters. ACB aims to keep CIR at 50% to invest in technology and digital banking, attracting CASA deposits, with CIR expected to decrease to 46-48% by 2022.

Non-performing loans from 6 companies related to Mr. Nguyen Duc Kien total VND 806 billion (fully provisioned, with highly liquid collateral of VND 2.0 trillion), including VND 1.0 trillion in interest. Recovery is expected in FY20-21.

Shareholders approved a plan to issue international bonds, which ACB believes will diversify its funding sources, particularly USD, to serve import-export customers. This plan is likely to be implemented in 2021.

recommendation.justification: BVSC continues to maintain an OUTPERFORM recommendation for DHC with a target price of VND 50,000 per share based on the DCF method, as stated in our previous report dated 23/04/2020.

investment_thesis: DHC is suitable for long-term investors due to its strong financial foundation and abundant operating cash flow from Giao Long 2, which will help maintain a stable cash dividend policy in the future.
The company is expected to repay all medium-term debt in 2021, leading to savings in interest expenses.
The future production of Kraftliner paper and the development of Packaging Factory No. 1 will serve as additional growth drivers for the company.
company_update: DHC announced Q2 2020 revenue of VND 649 billion (+181% yoy) and net profit after tax (NPAT) of nearly VND 79 billion (+212% yoy), exceeding expectations. After 6 months, DHC completed 56% of its annual revenue plan and 84% of its annual profit plan.

DHC's paper consumption volume was not significantly affected by COVID-19, with a decrease only in April due to social distancing, followed by an almost full recovery in subsequent months. The Giao Long 2 factory is now operating at maximum capacity.

Input and output prices are stabilizing; input OCC (old corrugated carton) prices sharply decreased by 25% from their May 2020 peak after economies reopened, while output prices are gradually recovering to early 2020 levels.
The gross margin sharply decreased to 14.7% in Q2, primarily due to input prices peaking and output prices bottoming out simultaneously. This is considered a temporary effect.

Tax refunds in Q2 2020 significantly supported overall business results. The Giao Long 2 factory is exempt from tax for 2 years and receives a 50% tax reduction for the next 4 years, starting from early 2020. A tax refund of nearly VND 17 billion was recorded in Q2 due to provisional 20% tax calculation in Q1.
Testing and production of Kraftliner paper may be postponed to 2021 due to the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic, which has prevented DHC from inviting foreign experts for consultation.

Packaging Factory No. 1 is still under investment and will not face time pressure. Consumption at the existing packaging factory is still decreasing compared to the same period and is not operating at full capacity due to the COVID-19 pandemic.
analyst_viewpoints: BVSC believes that short-term momentum for DHC stock is limited, as the stock has already increased by over 20% since early July, reflecting positive Q2 profits and relief after major shareholders Daiwa and SSIAM successfully divested, and Giao Long 2 is operating at maximum capacity.
BVSC still considers DHC suitable for long-term investors.
Analysts expect gross margin to improve from the second half of Q3 2020 with current input/output price trends.
However, profit margins are unlikely to return to the high level of 24.2% seen in Q1 2020 because DHC was still utilizing historically low-priced inventory from Q4 2019 at that time.
Giao Long 2's full-year 2020 production volume is likely to reach 100% compared to BVSC's previous forecast of 77-80%.

Analysts expect OCC prices to continue a slight downward trend in the second half of 2020 and even into 2021, as China maintains a strict environmental stance and aims to stop relying on imported recovered paper.
Kraftliner is a higher-end product using both pulp and recycled OCC as inputs, so its selling price could be 20-30% higher than the company's current products.
DHC can leverage its existing Giao Long 2 production line and flexibly switch between products depending on market conditions.

risk_assessment.risk: COVID-19 pandemic impact on demand and project delays.
risk_assessment.impact: Consumption volume decreased in April due to social distancing, and the existing packaging factory is not operating at full capacity. The testing and production of Kraftliner paper may be postponed to 2021 due to difficulties in inviting foreign experts.
risk_assessment.mitigation: The company's paper consumption volume has largely recovered, and the Packaging Factory No. 1 project will not face time pressure, allowing flexibility in investment.

risk_assessment.risk: Input and output price volatility and margin compression.
risk_assessment.impact: Large volumes of goods from FDI enterprises facing difficulties exporting to China had to be sold domestically, dragging down output prices in Q2. The COVID-19 pandemic severely affected the supply of recovered OCC paper, leading to price increases. Gross margin sharply decreased in Q2, and profit margins are unlikely to return to Q1 2020's high levels due to the depletion of historically low-priced inventory.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Input and output prices are stabilizing, and gross margins are expected to improve from mid-Q3 2020 onwards. The company's ability to leverage Giao Long 2 for flexible product switching can help manage market conditions.

key_tracking: Progress of the Kraftliner paper production project
Progress of Packaging Factory No. 1 project
Tax policy for Giao Long 2 factory (exemption/reduction)
other_info: The 52-week price range for DHC is VND 28,400-43,750.
DHC's market capitalization is VND 2,374 billion.
The number of outstanding shares is 55,995,783.
The 10-day average trading volume is 1,552,174 shares.
Foreign ownership percentage is 26.6%, with a foreign room of 49.9%.
The Beta coefficient for DHC is 0.9.

recommendation.justification: BVSC valued TCB using two methods: P/B and Residual Income, each weighted 50%. For the P/B method, BVSC applied a P/B of 1.13, which is the historical average median of ACB and MBB, two banks considered most similar to TCB in Vietnam. A 10% discount was applied to this median due to the impact of Covid-19 on Vietnam's economic growth. The target price derived from this valuation is VND 21,500 per share.

investment_thesis: BVSC favors Techcombank due to its sustained high growth over many years, with a 10-year compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 19.5% for profit.
Techcombank possesses an innovative culture and is a pioneer in developing new services to meet customer needs.
The bank demonstrates high operational efficiency, with an average Return on Equity (ROE) of 21.1% over the last four years, coupled with the lowest financial leverage in the system at 5.9 times.
company_update: Techcombank announced its Q2 2020 financial results with a profit after tax (NPAT) increase of 16.9% year-on-year (yoy), despite a sharp 6.1-fold yoy increase in provision expenses.

This positive NPAT was driven by a 26% yoy increase in net interest income, a 33.9% yoy increase in net fee income, and effective control over operating expenses, which reduced the Cost-to-Income Ratio (CIR) to 29.5%, the lowest level in the past seven quarters.

Credit growth was modest in the first six months of 2020, increasing 3.5% year-to-date (YTD), slightly higher than the system-wide average of 3.3%. Q1 2020 credit growth was 3.7% YTD, followed by a 0.2% quarter-on-quarter (QoQ) decrease in Q2 2020.
TCB's credit growth was primarily led by corporate bonds, which saw a 27% YTD increase, while customer loans increased by 0.5% QoQ in Q1 and decreased by 0.2% QoQ in Q2.
The low growth in customer loans is attributed to reduced credit demand due to the Covid-19 pandemic and fewer new real estate projects launched in 6M2020.

Management indicated that the bank has received approval to increase its credit room and expects credit growth to be more favorable in the second half of the year, though BVSC anticipates corporate bond holdings will continue to be a significant driver.

Customer deposits reached VND 250 trillion in Q2 2020, growing 8% YTD. TCB capitalized on low interest rates to increase capital mobilization, ensuring liquidity and capacity for future lending expansion.
The CASA (Current Account Savings Account) ratio stood at 34.4%, ranking second highest in the system after MBB, recovering from a slight seasonal dip in Q1.

TCB's leading online transaction platform, free transactions, and debit card cashback have attracted a large number of individual customers, boosting CASA. The bank is also pioneering a free platform for corporate customers, expected to further increase CASA and fee income.

Net Interest Margin (NIM) in Q2 2020 increased by 66 basis points (bps) yoy to 4.19% (BVSC estimate), positioning TCB as the third-highest NIM bank in the system (excluding VPB and HDB due to their significant consumer finance contributions). This NIM improvement was mainly due to a 54 bps yoy reduction in funding costs, driven by lower deposit interest rates and a higher CASA ratio.
Quarter-on-quarter, TCB's NIM decreased by 36 bps, as asset yield declined by 52 bps, a sharper drop than the 16 bps decrease in funding costs.

The decline in asset yield was due to TCB offering a VND 30 trillion credit package with preferential interest rates to support Covid-19 affected customers, restructured loans having interest income moved off-balance sheet and not yet recognized, and a general reduction in lending interest rates due to decreased demand.
BVSC forecasts TCB's NIM to be around 4.0% for the full year 2020.
